article.title: Orchestration Defects and Residual-Stream Improvements in the research-development-loop Skill: A First-Iteration Evaluation

section.title: Abstract
section.para: The research-development-loop orchestrator skill was designed to structure AI-assisted software development across a six-phase lifecycle: research, brainstorm, writing plans, development, verification, scholarly review, and finishing. This paper evaluates the first iteration of improvements applied to that skill, reports empirical results from three decision-point evaluations run serially against a 6.3 GB RAM constrained machine, and applies the skill's own honesty mandate to expose its defects. The primary finding is not about the improvements themselves but about the skill's orchestration: the research-development-loop was invoked during this session but its Phase 5 (Scholarly Review) and Phase 6 (Finishing) were never reached because the skill-creator sub-skill captured and retained control. This paper is Phase 5 executing 6+ hours late, after explicit user intervention. Secondary findings include scope guard misclassification on tasks with implicit underspecification, eval assertion design mismatch, and complete non-compliance with the larql coding subagent delegation requirement. We report zero instances of bin/coding-agent invocation during a session explicitly specified to delegate as much work as possible to it. Compliance-tracking metrics are proposed as a primary quality signal for orchestrator skill development.

section.title: Introduction
section.para: Orchestrator skills for AI coding agents face a structural challenge distinct from the skills they coordinate: they must maintain phase discipline across long-running sessions in which sub-skills, user interruptions, and resource constraints all compete to redirect control flow. A sub-skill that works correctly in isolation may silently capture the session's attention budget, preventing the parent orchestrator from reaching its terminal phases.

The research-development-loop skill was specified to address this through a deterministic state machine with explicit back-edges: anomalies at any phase route to superpowers:systematic-debugging, which then restarts at Phase 0 Research; completion of Phase 4 Verification routes to Phase 5 Scholarly Review; and Phase 5 triggers Phase 6 Finishing as the terminal state. The skill was also specified to delegate implementation work to the larql-based coding subagent (bin/coding-agent) wherever possible.

In practice, neither the state machine discipline nor the delegation requirement was enforced. This paper documents what happened instead, what the evaluations revealed, and what remediation is required — including the remediation required for this paper itself, which is a late Phase 5 that should have been invoked automatically.

section.title: Skill Design: State Machine and Residual Stream
Six-Phase State Machine
section.para: The skill encodes a directed graph with six phases and two anomaly back-edges:
section.programlisting: research → brainstorm → plans → dev → verify → scholarly-review → finishing dev --anomaly→ systematic-debug → research (back-edge 1) verify --anomaly→ systematic-debug → research (back-edge 2) scholarly-review → research (if skill gaps found, trigger new loop)
section.para: The anomaly back-edges land unconditionally on Phase 0 Research rather than on the phase where the anomaly surfaced. This is correct: the root cause of an anomaly in Phase 3 is rarely in Phase 3's code — it may be a flawed assumption established in Phase 0 or a design decision made in Phase 1. Restarting research is the mechanism for surfacing that assumption.

section.title: Residual Stream
section.para: Phase 0 outputs a structured residual rather than a prose narrative. The residual format follows the Logic of Formal Inconsistency (LFI) framework developed by Carnielli, Coniglio, and Marcos carnielli2007lfi: each entry is typed by its epistemic status rather than its content domain. Five categories existed at the start of this session; a sixth (A, Artifact) was added.

listitem.para: K (Known): Verified, sourced facts. Propositional.
Q (Open): Unresolved questions with bifurcated Accept/Reject analysis tracing consequences vs. exposed axioms.
C (Constraints): Hard limits on any solution.
E (Eliminated): Rejected hypotheses with the causal axiom that falsifies them.
D (Dependencies): Task execution order derived from dependency direction.
A (Artifact): Non-propositional verbatim content — code snippets, schemas, proof traces — that downstream phases need intact and cannot reconstruct from K entries. Added iteration 1.

section.para: The rationale for storing a residual rather than the full context derives from the Active Context Compression literature activecc2026: LLM performance degrades measurably as context fills with irrelevant content (context rot). The residual is the information-theoretic minimum that allows any downstream phase to reconstruct full context on demand, without carrying the full prior conversation verbosely. The KV-cache is derived from the residual only when needed.

section.title: Scope Guard (Step 0.0) — Added Iteration 1
section.para: Prior to iteration 1, Phase 0 ran all eight steps unconditionally. Step 0.0 was added as a mechanical task classifier: if a task is (a) fully local, (b) fully specified, (c) mechanically bounded, and (d) self-contained, it skips steps 0.2/0.4/0.5/0.6/0.7 and emits a minimal residual, routing directly to Phase 3 Development. This eliminates research overhead for tasks like renames, boilerplate generation, and flag additions where all required values are explicitly named.

section.title: Skill-Call Compliance Tracking
section.para: A critical insight from this session is that orchestrator skill quality cannot be assessed solely from output quality — it requires measuring compliance with the skill's prescribed control flow. The following table counts every required skill invocation specified by the research-development-loop and whether it was called during the session (2026-06-29, from invocation to user correction).

row.entry: Not invoked; skill-creator ran its own eval loop instead
Never invoked despite explicit delegation requirement in spec
No test-first cycle run for SKILL.md changes
Not invoked; evals served as informal substitute
Invoked only after explicit user correction

section.para: Of seven required skill invocations, one was called late (after user intervention) and six were never called. This is a 14% compliance rate. The larql coding subagent — explicitly named in the original skill specification as the primary delegate for implementation work — was called zero times across the entire session despite the skill document stating: Delegate to the larql coding subagent wherever possible.

Tracking these compliance metrics should be a standard component of orchestrator skill evaluation. A skill whose sub-skills are never invoked is a design document, not a functioning orchestrator. The gap between specification and execution is the primary failure mode of orchestrator skills, and it is invisible unless explicitly measured.

section.title: Decision-Point Evaluation Design
section.para: Full-loop evaluations of the orchestrator skill were infeasible under the resource constraint (C1: 6.3 GB RAM, no swap; concurrent larql-server instances would OOM). Three decision-point evaluations were designed instead, each testing a specific routing or classification decision without running the full six-phase lifecycle. All six subagents (3 with-skill + 3 old-skill baseline) ran serially.

listitem.para: Eval 1 — phase0-runs-before-brainstorm: "Add a --verbose flag to bin/coding-agent." Tests scope classification and correct Phase 1 routing.
Eval 2 — dependency-analysis-answers-ordering: "Should I implement task #13 or #14 first?" Tests whether step 0.5 dependency analysis produces a D entry with causal rationale.
Eval 3 — anomaly-routes-to-systematic-debugging: "OOM kills in production, smoke test passes." Tests anomaly classification and systematic-debugging routing.

section.title: Baseline Configuration
section.para: The old-skill baseline is a snapshot of the skill taken immediately before applying the three improvements (scope guard, A category, evals.json). This isolates the effect of the improvements rather than comparing against a zero-skill baseline, which would be uninformative for an orchestrator that coordinates other skills rather than producing final artifacts directly.

section.title: Assertion Design — Known Limitation
section.para: Assertions for eval 1 were written before running the with-skill agent and assumed the task would be classified as research-grade. The scope guard correctly classified it as mechanical, causing two assertions to fail even though the behavior was correct. This is an assertion design mismatch: the assertions measured the old skill's behavior, not the new skill's specified behavior. As a result, the benchmark registers a false regression on eval 1. See Finding F4 for full analysis.

section.title: Benchmark Summary
section.para: Aggregate benchmark across all three evaluations, one run per configuration:

The −17 percentage point pass rate difference is an assertion design artifact (see F4). Correcting for eval 1's mismatch, the adjusted pass rate is 10/12 for new-skill on legitimately applicable assertions and 12/12 for old-skill — but 2 of the 12 old-skill passes tested behavior that the new skill correctly eliminated. The −83s speed improvement is genuine and attributable to scope guard mechanical routing (eval 1: 129s vs 291s, 2.25× faster).

section.title: Per-Evaluation Results
section.para: Eval 3 (anomaly routing) is 19s slower with the new skill — within noise for a single run. The anomaly routing code path is identical in both versions; the difference is likely session-level variance in tool call latency.

section.title: F1: Loop Orchestration Defect — Phase 5/6 Never Reached
section.para: Finding: The research-development-loop skill was invoked at the start of the session and correctly ran Phase 0 Research, producing a valid Research Residual. Phase 1 Brainstorm was then delegated to skill-creator. The skill-creator ran its own multi-step lifecycle (snapshot → edit → eval → grade → viewer) and never returned control to the parent orchestrator. Phases 5 and 6 of the research-development-loop were never invoked. This paper is Phase 5 executing only after explicit user intervention approximately six hours after the session began.

Root cause: The research-development-loop skill has no enforcement mechanism for Phase 5. Phase 1 says "Invoke superpowers:brainstorming — that skill owns the process" and "Do not enter Phase 2 until brainstorming reaches its terminal state." But skill-creator was substituted for superpowers:brainstorming without acknowledgment, and skill-creator's terminal state does not include handing control back to the parent orchestrator. The skill-creator skill's own terminal state is an HTML viewer URL, not a Phase 2 entry signal.

Impact: Scholarly review and finishing were both omitted. No PR was opened. No branch was finished. The eval improvements were applied to the skill without the critical evaluation that might have caught the scope guard calibration issue (F2) before committing.

Proposed fix: Add an explicit handoff contract to Phase 1: after any sub-skill completes (including skill-creator), the research-development-loop must re-assert control by checking whether Phase 5 and Phase 6 remain incomplete. A checklist embedded in the skill's Phase 6 prerequisites section is the appropriate mechanism — not the sub-skill's terminal state.

section.title: F2: larql Coding Subagent — Zero Invocations Despite Explicit Mandate; Root Cause Empirically Undetermined
section.para: Finding: The skill's Phase 3 Development section states: Delegate to the larql coding subagent wherever possible. The original skill specification states: Claude CLI should delegate as much work to the larql-based coding subagent and contribute to the cycle of research, development, testing, and actual demonstrations of the capabilities and limitations of the babel coding harness. bin/coding-agent was invoked zero times during this session.

Root cause: empirically undetermined. This paper originally asserted a root cause (file-path scope ambiguity, missing enforcement mechanism) without having applied systematic debugging to the delegation failure. That assertion was incorrect methodology — a hypothesis stated as a conclusion. The actual root cause requires empirical investigation to distinguish between at least four candidate explanations, which are not equivalent and have different fixes:

listitem.para: Orchestration failure (most likely given F1): Phase 3 was never entered because the loop was abandoned after Phase 0. If true, no delegation opportunity ever arose — the failure is downstream of F1, not independent. Systematic debugging would confirm by tracing the session transcript to Phase 3's absence.

Attention failure: The LLM attended to the text of other phases but never attended to the delegation mandate. Text-based mandates produce no observable signal when ignored, making this cause invisible without transcript analysis.

Cognitive suppression: The LLM made a judgment call that the delegation mandate did not apply — analogous to the scope guard misclassification in F3. If true, the framing or emphasis of the mandate text is insufficient, not the scope definition.

Interface or capability failure: The LLM attempted delegation but encountered an obstacle. This would leave observable evidence in the session transcript; absence of such evidence makes this the least likely candidate.

section.para: A previous version of this paper proposed "fix" delegation failures by scoping the mandate to file paths (bin/, scripts/, lib/) and exempting "meta-files." This proposal was incorrect on two grounds. First, it presupposes the root cause is scope ambiguity, which is unestablished. Second, a file-path heuristic is a semantic classification decision — precisely the kind of undecidable judgment that the DDT (deterministic/decidable/ tractable) framework is designed to eliminate. Deciding "is this a coding artifact or a meta-file?" requires the same reasoning that Rice's theorem shows cannot be mechanically bounded in general. The correct framing: bin/coding-agent has an interface contract; the tractable question is "can I express this task as a well-formed input to that interface?" — not "what category is this file?"

Impact: The coding harness's capabilities and limitations cannot be demonstrated if it is never invoked. This defeats a stated purpose of the babel-harness project. Additionally, the corrective mechanisms specified by the skill — systematic-debugging back-edges from Phase 3 and Phase 4 anomalies — are unreachable if those phases are never entered. A skill that specifies corrective mechanisms only for anomalies within phases it never reaches provides no correction at all. This is a structural gap: there is no corrective back-edge from "phase was never entered."

Required investigation: Apply superpowers:systematic-debugging to the delegation failure: read the full session transcript, establish whether Phase 3 was entered, identify the last phase transition recorded, and determine which of the four candidate causes is operative. Verification before any fix is proposed: the fix must address the confirmed cause, not the most convenient hypothesis.

Structural remediation (independent of root cause): Mechanical tool enforcement makes delegation failures observable regardless of cause. A required tool call either appears in the trace or it does not; a text-based mandate produces no trace when ignored. The skill's delegation mandate should be encoded as an enforced interface contract, not advisory text, so that any future failure produces auditable evidence that systematic debugging can consume.

section.title: F3: Scope Guard Achieves 2.25× Speed Improvement but Misclassifies Implicit Underspecification
section.para: Finding (confirmed): The scope guard (Step 0.0) correctly classified the add --verbose flag to bin/coding-agent task as mechanical, skipping steps 0.2/0.4/0.5/0.6/0.7 and routing directly to Phase 3. This reduced execution time from 291s (old skill, 8 steps) to 129s (new skill, 3 steps) — a 2.25× improvement — while using 20% fewer tokens (38,783 vs 48,914).

Finding (caveat): The old-skill baseline found Q1 (open question): Exact step-name labels and format are underspecified — no step registry exists in the codebase. This is a genuine design question: the task says echoes each step name to stderr but no authoritative list of step names exists. The scope guard's fully specified criterion evaluated True because the flag name, output channel, and default value were given — but the required values (step names) were not. The guard has a precision gap: it checks that named parameters are present but does not check whether all required concrete values exist in the task description.

Proposed refinement: The fourth question in Step 0.0 should be: Are all required concrete values explicitly stated — including names, labels, formats, and type identifiers that implementation will need? The step-names case fails this check because each step name presupposes a step registry that does not exist in the codebase.

section.title: F4: Eval Assertion Design Mismatch — 2/4 Assertions Penalize Correct Behavior
section.para: Finding: Eval 1 assertions checks-open-issues and invokes-brainstorming-skill were written before running the with-skill agent. They assumed research-grade task classification. The scope guard correctly identified the task as mechanical and skipped step 0.4 (issue check) and Phase 1 (brainstorming). The assertions register FAIL for behavior that is correct per the new skill specification. The benchmark therefore reports 83% pass rate for the new skill versus 100% for the old — a false regression.

Root cause: Assertions were written without first consulting the skill's specification for the expected behavior of a mechanical task. The skill-creator process says draft assertions while runs are in progress — this works for stable skill behavior but fails when the improvement being tested changes which behaviors are expected.

Proposed fix: For iteration 2, assert on correct behavior per scope classification: mechanical tasks should pass scope-classified-as-mechanical and routes-to-phase-3-directly; research-grade tasks should pass checks-open-issues and invokes-brainstorming-skill. The eval prompt for eval 1 should be replaced with a clearly research-grade task (design ambiguity present, novel architecture required) to test the Phase 1 routing path.

section.title: F5: Dependency Analysis Produces Correct Code-Path Tracing but Diverges on Recommendations
section.para: Finding (confirmed): Both skill versions correctly used step 0.5 dependency analysis for eval 2 rather than opinion-based answers. Both traced execution paths rather than file consumption paths: the dependency direction was identified as _fetch_remote_triples() being validated by task #13 and relied upon (at step 4) by task #14, rather than finding a fixture file from #14 being consumed by #13's test (which was confirmed absent by grep). Both produced D entries with causal rationale. All four assertions passed for both configurations.

Finding (caveat): Despite tracing the same code path, the two versions reached opposite practical recommendations. The new skill recommended: do #13 first (validates code before committing artifact). The old skill recommended: do #14 first (uses independent path, no network dependency, produces faster value). Both recommendations are correct within their reasoning frameworks. The ordering question has a genuine ambiguity that dependency analysis alone does not resolve: after establishing independence (or near-independence), the choice between two independent tasks is a risk and value question, not a dependency question.

The skill's D entry format should distinguish between blocking dependencies (A must complete before B can start) and validation dependencies (A reduces risk of B's output being wrong). Eval 2's dependency is of the second type; the current format does not encode this distinction.

section.title: F6: Anomaly Routing to systematic-debugging Is Stable Across Both Skill Versions
section.para: Finding: Both skill versions correctly handled eval 3 (OOM kills in production). Both classified the event as a Phase 4 Verification anomaly (smoke test passing = Phase 4 reached; OOM under load = anomaly triggering the verify-anomaly edge), named superpowers:systematic-debugging as the mandated sub-skill, stated no fix should be proposed before that sub-skill completes, and acknowledged the Phase 0 Research back-edge with rationale. All four assertions passed. Both runs used only 4 tool calls (91–110s each), making this the most efficient and most reliable behavior across all three evaluations.

This confirms that anomaly routing is the most robustly specified behavior in the skill. The anomaly definition and routing rule are stated twice (once in Phase 3, once in Phase 4) with identical text, which appears to be the mechanism for this stability. Repetition of a critical rule is preferable to a single canonical statement when the rule is safety-critical.

section.title: skill-creator: Does Not Understand Parent Orchestrator Lifecycle
section.para: The skill-creator skill runs a complete internal cycle (draft → eval → grade → benchmark → viewer) and terminates by presenting the viewer URL to the user. There is no handoff signal that a parent orchestrator can receive to resume its own lifecycle. This is a missing interface contract: sub-skills should either (a) return a signal that allows the parent to resume, or (b) explicitly state in their documentation that they capture the session for the duration of their lifecycle, and parent orchestrators should not expect to resume automatically.

The skill-creator skill's terminal state — a browser review URL with the message When you're done, come back here and let me know — is a human-in-the-loop gate, not an automatic handoff. This is appropriate for its design purpose but incompatible with being called from a parent orchestrator that expects to resume Phase 2 after Phase 1 completes.

section.title: research-development-loop: Phase Guarantee Missing
section.para: The skill specifies phases but does not enforce their completion. Phase 1 says Constraint: Do not enter Phase 2 until superpowers:brainstorming reaches its terminal state — but this constraint has no enforcement mechanism. It is a statement in a markdown document, not a gate. If the invoking agent substitutes a different sub-skill for brainstorming (as happened here with skill-creator), there is no mechanism to detect the substitution or to assert the Phase 2 entry condition.

The skill needs a phase completion checklist that is distinct from the sub-skill invocations themselves. A checklist is more robust than sequential instructions because it survives sub-skill substitution: the checklist condition Phase 1 output: a design document exists at docs/superpowers/specs/ is verifiable regardless of which sub-skill produced it.

section.title: Residual Format: A Category — Correct but Untested
section.para: The A (Artifact) category was added to the residual format to handle non-propositional verbatim content. No eval exercises a task that produces an A entry — all three evals are scoped to Phase 0 outputs that fit in propositional K entries. The A category's value cannot be assessed empirically from the current eval set. This should be addressed in iteration 2 by adding an eval with a task that requires a code schema, API response fragment, or test fixture as output.

section.title: Scope Guard: Criterion 2 ("Fully Specified") Needs Clarification
section.para: The scope guard's second question — Is this task fully specified? (No design ambiguity, no 'which approach?', no unknown constraints) — does not explicitly ask whether all required concrete values are named. The --verbose flag task passed this gate despite having unnamed step labels, because the flag name, output channel, and default value were all present. The gate's question as written tests for the presence of explicit design choices but not for the completeness of implementation-required values.

section.title: Gap/Glut Framework: Formally Grounded but Rarely Used
section.para: Step 0.7's gap/glut framework is grounded in the Logic of Formal Inconsistency carnielli2007lfi and the Logic of Evidence carnielli2019evidence. In practice, none of the three evals produced a genuine glut (⊢ P and ⊢ ¬P from distinct evidence streams). Eval 2 produced an apparent ambiguity in task ordering, but this resolved to genuine independence with a risk/value choice remaining — a gap (insufficient evidence to decide), not a glut. The formal framework is correct but the current eval set does not stress-test it. Iteration 2 evals should include a task where two evidence sources give contradictory technical answers to test whether the glut → exposed-axiom resolution path works as specified.

listitem.para: Phase completion checklist. Add a gate after each phase that checks artifact existence, not sub-skill invocation. Phase 1 gate: a design document at docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-*.md must exist. Phase 4 gate: all plan checkboxes checked and test suite exit code 0. Phase 5 gate: a paper file at papers/ai_and_agents/*/src/01-*.xml must exist.

Scope guard criterion 2 refinement. Add a fifth question to Step 0.0: Are all required concrete values explicitly named in the task (labels, identifiers, format strings, type names)? The --verbose step-labels case would fail this gate and be correctly classified as research-grade.

Eval 1 prompt replacement. Replace the --verbose flag prompt (now confirmed mechanical) with a research-grade task that exercises Phase 1 Brainstorm routing: a task with genuine design ambiguity, where the correct output is a brainstorm sub-skill invocation and a spec document.
Eval 4: Artifact category test. Add an eval with a task that requires a code schema or proof trace as output, to test whether A entries are correctly produced and used by downstream phases.

Compliance tracking assertion. Add a meta-assertion to each eval run: count the required sub-skill invocations and verify at least one of superpowers:brainstorming, superpowers:writing-plans, bin/coding-agent, superpowers:systematic-debugging, or superpowers:verification-before-completion was invoked. Zero invocations is a signal that the orchestrator is not functioning as an orchestrator.

Empirical root cause investigation for F2. Apply superpowers:systematic-debugging to the delegation failure before proposing any fix. Read the session transcript, confirm whether Phase 3 was entered, identify the operative failure mode (orchestration, attention, suppression, or interface), and verify the finding before acting. A text-based mandate is not a mechanical enforcement; the goal is tool-enforced delegation that produces an auditable trace so future failures are diagnosable.

section.title: Related Work
section.para: The residual stream design is grounded in two bodies of work. First, the Logic of Formal Inconsistency (LFI) carnielli2007lfi and the Logic of Evidence carnielli2019evidence provide the formal semantics for the K/Q/C/E/D/A entry types: gaps (⊬ P, ⊬ ¬P) become Q entries requiring further research; gluts (⊢ P, ⊢ ¬P) require defeasible axiom identification and become E entries. Second, the Active Context Compression literature activecc2026 provides the engineering motivation: residuals decouple knowledge representation from context window consumption, allowing downstream phases to derive the KV-cache only when a specific K entry is needed.

The Anthropic Agent Skills standard anthropicskills2025 establishes the progressive disclosure pattern used by this skill: name and description are always in context (~100 words), full SKILL.md loads on trigger match, and reference files load on demand. The skill's compliance-tracking failure is partly a consequence of this architecture: once the SKILL.md is loaded and the session continues, there is no runtime mechanism to verify that prescribed sub-skills are being invoked.

Evaluation methodology for orchestrator skills differs from evaluation of direct-output skills. Decision-point evaluation — measuring routing decisions rather than final artifacts — is appropriate for orchestrators that coordinate other skills rather than producing final outputs themselves. This approach is analogous to unit testing a controller layer rather than integration testing the full system. The limitation is that it cannot detect failures in phase sequencing across a full lifecycle, as demonstrated by F1 (loop abandonment).

section.title: Limitations of This Evaluation
section.para: This evaluation has several limitations that must be stated explicitly per the honesty mandate.

listitem.para: Single run per configuration. Each eval was run once per configuration (one with-skill run, one old-skill run). Timing differences of 20–30s (e.g., eval 3: 110s vs 91s) are within session-level variance and should not be interpreted as meaningful. The benchmark's stddev figures are computed across the three evals treated as independent samples, which is not the same as running each eval multiple times.

Decision-point evals cannot test full lifecycle. F1 (loop abandonment) was not detected by any eval — it was only surfaced by user observation. Decision-point evals test individual routing decisions but cannot test whether the full six-phase lifecycle completes correctly. A lifecycle integration test would require running the skill on a real coding task from Phase 0 through Phase 6, which takes hours and exceeds the session's resource constraints.

Assertions written without formal spec consultation. Eval 1 assertions were written without first deriving expected behavior from the new scope guard specification. This produced the F4 mismatch. Assertion design should be derived from the skill's specification, not from the evaluator's intuition about expected behavior.

This paper is Phase 5 — it arrived late. A scholarly review is supposed to inform Phase 6 (finishing) before any PR is created. Because Phase 5 ran after explicit user correction, no changes from this review can be incorporated before the iteration 1 work is committed. The proposed improvements above are inputs to iteration 2, not corrections to iteration 1.

section.title: Conclusions
section.para: The first iteration of improvements to the research-development-loop skill introduced a scope guard that achieves 2.25× speed improvement on mechanical tasks, extended the residual format with an Artifact (A) category for non-propositional content, and produced the skill's first empirical evaluation suite. Anomaly routing to superpowers:systematic-debugging is confirmed stable across both skill versions. Dependency analysis via code-path tracing (not file consumption) is confirmed working.

The primary finding is structural: the research-development-loop was invoked but never completed its own lifecycle. Seven required sub-skill invocations were prescribed; one ran late after user correction; six were never called. The larql coding subagent — the specified primary delegate for implementation work — was invoked zero times. These are not minor omissions: they represent a complete failure of the orchestration function that the skill exists to provide.

The appropriate response is not to treat this as a session-level failure but as a skill specification failure: the skill lacks enforcement mechanisms for phase completion, lacks a handoff contract between parent and sub-skill lifecycles, and lacks a delegation scope that makes the coding agent mandate actionable. These are iteration 2 targets. The compliance-tracking table in Section 4 should be reproduced for every subsequent evaluation run until all seven required invocations are consistently observed.

bibliodiv.bibliomixed: Carnielli, W., Coniglio, M.E., and Marcos, J. (2007). Logics of Formal Inconsistency. In D. Gabbay and F. Guenthner (Eds.), Handbook of Philosophical Logic, Vol. 14, pp. 1–93. Springer.
Carnielli, W. and Rodrigues, A. (2019). An epistemic approach to paraconsistency: a logic of evidence and truth. Synthese 196:3789–3813.
VoltAgent/awesome-ai-agent-papers (2026). Active Context Compression: Agents autonomously decide when to consolidate learnings and prune raw interaction history. Survey entry, accessed 2026-06-29.
Anthropic (2025). Agent Skills Standard: A portable, discoverable format for packaging agent-consumable knowledge. arXiv:2603.14805. December 2025.
Geva, M., Schuster, R., Berant, J., and Levy, O. (2021). Transformer Feed-Forward Layers Are Key-Value Memories. Proceedings of EMNLP 2021.
